statusLine: EU fuel security is under strain with 16 of 27 countries below mandatory minimums in at least one fuel type, though overall regional averages remain above safety thresholds.
fullAnalysis:
The European Union's fuel security picture presents a mixed but concerning outlook as of March 2026. While EU-wide averages remain comfortably above the mandatory 90-day minimum across all fuel types—petrol at 110.1 days, diesel at 100.6 days, and jet fuel at 95.1 days—the distribution reveals significant vulnerabilities. A total of 16 of 27 EU countries are operating below the mandatory minimum in at least one critical fuel category, indicating widespread strain on national reserves.

The most severe shortfalls are concentrated in Central and Eastern Europe, with Romania facing critical shortages across all three fuel types (petrol 33.2 days, diesel 32.4 days, jet fuel 76.4 days) and Poland similarly exposed (petrol 66.6 days, diesel 74.7 days, jet fuel 25.3 days). Jet fuel reserves present the most widespread concern, with 18 countries below the 90-day threshold, including major economies like Germany (64.7 days), France (67.5 days), and Spain (42.1 days). This vulnerability in aviation fuel could significantly impact both commercial aviation and military readiness.

The fuel price environment reflects broader supply pressures, with Brent crude oil at $101.29 per barrel—a 39.7% increase since late February's Middle East supply disruptions began. Consumer prices vary dramatically across the bloc, from Malta's €1.34 per liter for petrol to the Netherlands' €2.389 per liter, suggesting uneven market dynamics and differing national fuel tax policies. The combination of depleted strategic reserves following recent emergency releases and sustained high oil prices creates a challenging backdrop for rebuilding adequate stock levels.
keyPoints: 16 of 27 EU countries are below mandatory 90-day minimums in at least one fuel type, despite regional averages appearing adequate
Romania and Poland face the most severe exposure with critical shortages across all three fuel categories
Jet fuel presents the broadest vulnerability with 18 countries below safety thresholds, including major economies Germany, France, and Spain
Brent crude has surged 39.7% since late February to $101.29/barrel, complicating efforts to rebuild depleted strategic reserves
Consumer fuel prices show extreme variation across the bloc, ranging from €1.34/L in Malta to €2.389/L in the Netherlands for petrol
